finding: Subjective perception of low relative standing amplifies motivated acquisition of high-calorie resources independent of actual resource scarcity, replicating adaptive allocation patterns seen across social species but producing maladaptive outcomes in resource-rich contexts.

Message framing alignment with audience temporal orientation (past vs. future) mediates ideological divergence in belief update: past-focused comparison format bridges political divide by reducing mismatch between evidence schema and recipient worldview.

Extrinsic incentive saliency shifts decision-making mindset away from intrinsic motivation toward calculative cost-benefit reasoning, reducing target behavior—effect driven by attention reallocation rather than self-signal dilution, strongest among intrinsically motivated agents.

Implicit facial trait inference biases both selection and evaluation of scientific information: competence and morality increase perceived quality; attractiveness increases initial interest but decreases perceived rigor, suggesting dual aesthetic/epistemic evaluation channels.

Belief in free will amplifies causal attribution to internal dispositions over situational constraints via perceived intentional control; this correspondence-bias amplification explains increased prescribed punishment and reward, independent of locus of control or judgment certainty.

Punishment's immediate cost creates asymmetric learning vulnerability in flexible agents: reward-learning victims struggle more than thieves to acquire beneficial costly behavior, driving evolutionary selection for rigid punishment preference over flexible learning in that role.
